date: 2026-06-18
video_id: 1bOW_OFOBak
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1bOW_OFOBak
title: The Macro Minute: How long will the Fed pretend to be hawkish?

Happy Thursday out there team 42, it's your skipper here at Darius Dale to present our macro minute for Thursday, June 18th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead off morning note, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, how long will the Fed pretend to be hawkish? The short answer is 6 to 9 months tops. On net, the results of Fed chair Kevin Warsh's task forces are likely to produce a dovish policy path over time, departing from the high conviction hawkish forward guidance outlined in the latest dot plot. In short, expect today's hawkish Fed to give way to a dovish Fed in two to three quarters based on Warsh's perhaps unintentional forward guidance of concrete policy proposals by year end. In terms of analytical nuance, here's how we currently view the outcome of each task force based on our deep understanding of the associated data and interconnected economic and geopolitical risks. Number one, communications. Fewer, more impactful Fed communications over time may inflate term premium in the bond market. Number two, regarding the balance sheet, a hawkish reduction from the current ample reserves framework will likely be accompanied by a dovish easing of bank regulation with the net result that is likely only marginally hawkish from the perspective of financial stability. Number three, with regard to the data task force, timely data will likely lead to a marginally dovish interpretation of the labor market. And number four, regarding the productivity and jobs task force, a marginally dovish interpretation of the labor market will boost estimates of productivity growth. And number five, regarding the inflation task force, the increased focus on real-time indicators from the private sector and trim mean measures will likely result in a marginally dovish interpretation of inflation. Regarding each of these task force forces, it's worth highlighting the following. Number one, the current level of the 10-year Treasury term premium is 59 basis points according to the ACM model, which is well shy of the 188 basis point mean since the prior to the GFC. This means the fair value of the 10-year nominal Treasury yield is roughly 5.75%. Yes, I said 5.75%. Number two, there is ample scope for the US commercial banks to be financially repressed into supporting the marketable treasury market. Their current share of 15% is effectively an all-time low and well shy of their peak 33% share in June 2003. Investors should expect the hot potato to be passed from the private non-bank sector's 58% share up from 36% at year-end 2021 to the US commercial banking sector in the coming years. Number three, the trailing three-year average QCEW benchmark revision in non-farm payrolls is minus 549,000 with 861,000 minus 861,000 coming in 2025. Number four, historically more productivity growth equals lower inflation and more scope for the Fed to ease the policy rate by a statistically significant degree. And number five, Trueflation's headline and core PCE estimates have lagged their corresponding BEA statistics by an average of 24 basis points and 28 basis points respectively since 2023. The latest reading of the Dallas Fed trim mean PCE deflator is up only 2.3% year-over-year, dramatically underperforming the 3.3% year-over-year rate of core PCE. All told, we were as prescient as any firm on global Wall Street to consistently highlight the risk of a surprise surprisingly hawkish Fed pivot dating back to late January and increasingly so in recent months. While this too shall pass, investors should not assume that our long-term sanguine take on US monetary policy means asset markets are currently de-risked from the perspective of the rising probability of a 1998-style severe correction followed by a V-bottom recovery over the medium term. Respect the x-axis. As always, we wrap up with a question from our community. Uh in our in the 42 macro dashboard, this one's titled shout out to DD. Just wanted to give DD his flowers in regards to his June 12th lead-off morning note. I know it's early, but with SpaceX reporting it looking to issue 20 billion in bonds, we may have seen that SpaceX hit its highs uh mid-week. Uh not a bad call, my guy. Uh so, if you recall in our June 12th lead-off morning note, we titled the note "How many days uh do investors have uh to how many days do traders have uh uh uh to to to I forget the title, but it was basically how many days do traders have to make money in SpaceX?" Uh we said approximately three with an anticipation of this hawkish uh quote-unquote hawk surprisingly hawkish Fed pivot. Uh uh it wasn't surprising to us. We had consistently highlighted three separate orthogonal vectors that were separate and apart from the Middle Eastern conflict with regards to uh the prospect for tighter Fed policy. Uh obviously not obviously, apparently it wasn't obvious, but uh we were in some dangerous straits also from the perspective of the monetary the the the mon the the monetary cycle in terms of the the amount of money being created in the economy. Uh the policy of fiscal policy and monetary policy, we think about the Fed monetization, were combining with uh dramatically eased bank lending uh in a way that was you know, inflating upside risk to inflation as well. And then you also have this massive aggregate demand shock uh from A cap X uh that is obviously impacting uh both the aggregate demand in the economy and also inflation statistics. And by the way, uh Apple just signaled today that they're going to be raising prices on uh iPads, uh Macs, Mac minis, and and then ultimately um your iPhone uh uh in the coming months uh uh as a function of this massive AI uh aggregate demand shock. So, uh you know, the Fed is is is right in our opinion to tight monetary policy. It's the right thing to do, uh but ultimately we do believe this this period of tight monetary tighter monetary policy will ultimately give way uh to a much more dovish Fed uh in uh about two to three week quarters time. Uh so, investors, you know, need to just, you know, prepare for that. Respect the X axis. If Kiss and Dr. Mo tell you to take down risk, you start taking down risk. When they tell you to start taking back up risk, because I don't think we're at the end of this 42 macro paradigm C bull market, then you uh happily take back up risk because I think this thing is going to get you up at some point, but probably not from this starting point. So, we'll wrap it up there. Darius Dale here presenting our macro minute for Thursday, June 18th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We won't catch you back here tomorrow. Tomorrow's June 10th, so we're going to be offline celebrating that wonderful holiday. So, catch you back here on Monday. Have a great weekend. Cheers.
